coredata.dc:title: Inhibition of the fishy odor from boiled crab meatballs during storage via novel combination of radio frequency and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Linlin
Zhang, Min
Wang, Haixiang
coredata.dc:description: Use of combination of radio frequency (RF) and carbon dots (CDs) was performed to pasteurize and reduce the fishy odor from boiled crab meatballs during storage. Due to the best pasteurization effect and relatively short treatment time, CDs combined RF heating for 30 min (CDRF30) was selected as the optimal CDRF treatment. The efficacy of CDRF30 was evaluated and compared to those of CDs treatment and high pressure steam (HPS) treatment. After stored for 15 days at 4 °C, the trimethylamine (TMA) concentration in CDRF30 group (80.95 μg/kg) was remarkably lower than those in control and CDs group; the total concentration of pentanal, hexanal, heptanal, and decanal (200.99 μg/kg), which were contributed to fishy odor in CDRF30 group were remarkably lower than those in control group, CDs group and HPS group. The results indicated that CDRF treated for 30 min can be a new pasteurization method for inhibiting the formation of fishy odor from boiled crab meatballs during refrigerated storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Radio frequency
Flavor
TMA
Lipid oxidation
GC-MS

Determination of thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS)
Determination of total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N)
Variation of pentanal, hexanal, heptanal, and decanal

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The colony forming units (CFUs) in samples with different treatments, (b) Center and surface temperature curves of the samples during RF heating.
(a) Variation of L*, a*, and b* values of meatball samples with different treatment, (b) Photos of meatball samples, and (c) Color variation of crab roe with different treatment.
(a) Response values of different crab meatballs, and (b) Principal component analysis for different crab meatballs by electronic nose.
(a) Changes of POV value, and (b) Changes of TBARS value in different crab meatball samples during storage.
(a) Changes of TVB-N value, and (b) Changes of TMA value in different crab meatball samples during storage.

row.entry: Liquefied petroleum gas, natural gas, gas

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Volatile components in boiled crab meat samples with different treatment.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Different lowercase letters within a line mean significantly different (P < 0.05). Numbers in brackets are kinds of volatile organic compounds of samples.
Note: Different lowercase letters within a column mean significantly different (P < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Variation of pentanal, hexanal, heptanal, and decanal in crab meat samples during storage.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Different lowercase letters within a line mean significantly different (P < 0.05). “-” means not detected. RT means retention time.

head.ce:title: Inhibition of the fishy odor from boiled crab meatballs during storage via novel combination of radio frequency and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, 214122, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, 214122, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Province Key Laboratory of Advanced Food Manufacturing Equipment and Technology, Jiangnan University, 214122, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Province Key Laboratory of Advanced Food Manufacturing Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Province Key Laboratory of Advanced Food Manufacturing Equipment and Technology, Jiangnan University, 214122, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yechun Food Production and Distribution Co., Ltd, 225000, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yechun Food Production and Distribution Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yechun Food Production and Distribution Co., Ltd., 225000, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Use of combination of radio frequency (RF) and carbon dots (CDs) was performed to pasteurize and reduce the fishy odor from boiled crab meatballs during storage. Due to the best pasteurization effect and relatively short treatment time, CDs combined RF heating for 30 min (CDRF30) was selected as the optimal CDRF treatment. The efficacy of CDRF30 was evaluated and compared to those of CDs treatment and high pressure steam (HPS) treatment. After stored for 15 days at 4 °C, the trimethylamine (TMA) concentration in CDRF30 group (80.95 μg/kg) was remarkably lower than those in control and CDs group; the total concentration of pentanal, hexanal, heptanal, and decanal (200.99 μg/kg), which were contributed to fishy odor in CDRF30 group were remarkably lower than those in control group, CDs group and HPS group. The results indicated that CDRF treated for 30 min can be a new pasteurization method for inhibiting the formation of fishy odor from boiled crab meatballs during refrigerated storage.

ce:section.ce:para: Boiled crab meatball is a famous traditional Chinese dish, because its plump shape looks like the head of a male lion, so it is named “lion head” (W. Zhu et al., 2021). The main raw materials of crab meatballs are lean pork meat, back pork fat and a certain amount of crab meat and crab roe (ovaries and digestive glands in the crab), and the surface is decorated with crab roe. During the factory process, the boiled crab meatballs were sterilized using high pressure steam (HPS) to extend the shelf life of products. However, after being treated with high temperature and pressure, the crab meatball sample have a strong fishy odor generated after cooling. Flavor is an important indicator to assess the sensory quality of crab meatballs. The volatile compounds present in crab meatball products include some pleasant odorants; however, they also include some unpleasant off-odorants responsible for fishy odor of crab and pork. The fishy odor greatly reduces the consumer's acceptance of products, therefore, it is very important to reduce the fishy odors of crab meatballs.

During processing and storage, fishy odorants are mainly generated through the decomposition of trimethylamine oxide (TMAO) (Park et al., 2020) and lipid oxidation (Y. Thiansilakul, Benjakul, Park, & Richards, 2012). TMAO is an endogenous substance that naturally exists in aquatic products, which has no smell but has umami taste. Under the action of anaerobic microorganisms/enzymes, TMAO is easily degraded to produce TMA, resulting in fishy odor (T. H. Wu & Bechtel, 2008). High temperature (J. Zhu, Li, & Jia, 2012) and high pressure (Erkan et al., 2011) can also promote the degradation of TMAO.

The fat and crab roe in the crab meatballs contain unsaturated fatty acids, which easy to be oxidized. Hydroperoxides are produced during enzymatic or automatic oxidation of unsaturated fatty acids, it can be further degraded to produce some small volatile substances such as aldehydes, ketones, alcohols, etc., and the generation of these secondary oxidation products is the main reason for the formation of fishy odor (Thiansilakul et al., 2010). Therefore, it is necessary to develop a mild pasteurization technology, which could effectively inhibit the production of fishy odorants caused by lipid oxidation, and inhibit the formation of TMA caused by high temperature and high pressure degradation or microbial spoilage of TMAO while ensuring the microbial safety of products.

In recent years, in order to extend the shelf life and improve the quality of packaged dishes, studies have focused on new preservation techniques. Radio frequency (RF) pasteurization is a relatively new technology in food industry and is increasingly being recognized as an alternative technique to tradition sterilization methods like high pressure steam (HPS) sterilization. Compared with the traditional heating method, RF uses electromagnetic effect to heat materials, relies less on conduction mode of heat transfer due to which the temperature distribution within the material becomes more uniform and the heating time become shorter (Roknul, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2014; Wang, Zhang, & Mujumdar, 2014; Zhang, Tang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2006; Zhang, Zhang, Shan, & Fang, 2007). The electromagnetic effect and thermal effect of RF make it have an excellent effect of killing harmful microorganisms. Its abilities of bacteria and spores inactivation have been proved and reported (Jiao, Zhang, Hu, & Zhao, 2019). Compared with HPS sterilization, RF treatment causes less loss of product quality (Xu, Zhang, Cao, Adhikari, & Yang, 2019).

Carbon dots (CDs) is a kind of zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles with a diameter less than 10 nm (L. Zhao, Zhang, Bhandari, & Bai, 2020). Depending on their surface functional groups, defects, edge shapes, and sizes, CDs exhibit unique physico-chemical properties (Sharma, Das, & Yun, 2020). It was also reported to be non-toxic (Mehta, Jha, & Kailasa, 2014). As for their applications in food processing and preservation, CDs have been found their uses as potent antimicrobial agents and antioxidants. Fan, Zhang, Fan, and Jiang (2019) reported that CDs inhibited the growth of yeasts and molds. Our previous studies have demonstrated that CDs could effectively inhibit the growth of selected bacteria, including Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli and Bacillus subtilis (L. Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2020), also illustrated that CDs had superior capacities for scavenging superoxide anion, hydroxyl and 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) radicals, and exhibited potent inhibitory effect for lipid oxidation of frying oil and fried meatballs (L. Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2021). Using the characteristics of mild pasteurization of RF and the excellent antibacterial and antioxidant properties of CDs, the combination of both can be used as a new technique to suppress the production of fishy smell.

The novelty of this work resided on the combination of CDs and RF treatment for pasteurization and for inhibiting the formation of fishy odors during processing and storage of boiled crab meatballs. Microbial level, color changes, peroxide value (POV), thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS), and total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) contents of crab meatballs were monitored, and the changes of flavor, especially the changes of TMA level were analyzed. In addition, volatile compounds contributed to fishy odor were detected with a gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and preparation of boiled crab meatball samples
ce:section.ce:para: Materials for the preparation of boiled crab meatballs, including back pork fat (24%), lean pork meat (36%), crab meat (20%), crab roe (8%), onion powder (1.6%), salt (1%), soy sauce (1.2%), ginger powder (1.4%), white wine (1.2%), sugar (1.6%), and corn starch (4%) were supplied by Yechun Food Co., Ltd. (Yangzhou, China). CDs was fabricated in the Institute of Food Resource and Comprehensive Utilization of Jiangnan University, which was synthesized with banana paste using hydrothermal method, with the particle size was 0.54–1.50 nm. The CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray pattern, Raman, Fourier transfer infrared (FTIR), fluorescence and UV–visible spectroscopies, and its antioxidant and antibacterial properties were demonstrated.

The whole experiment was performed in triplicate with the same formulation and ingredients. Lean pork meat and back pork fat were first diced and then mixed with crab meat, crab roe, salt, onion powder, ginger powder, soy sauce, sugar, white wine, corn starch and water. The meat mixture was stirred in a clockwise direction and refrigerate at 4 °C for 1 h. Meatballs were shaped by hand (each weighed 40 ± 0.5 g), then heated in boiling water (100 °C) to shape and cooked for 12 min. After cooking, meatballs were cooled at room temperature (25 ± 5 °C) for 40 min. The samples were then packed in polyethylene terephthalate bags and vacuum-packed.

In this study, the CDs powder were dispersed in deionized water at a concentration of 2 mg/mL. Then the CDs dispersed water was added to the meat mixture. Eight groups of crab meatball samples were prepared, which were control group (without any treatment), HPS group (121 °C for 30 min), WB group (water bath, heating at 70 °C for 30 min), CDs group (addition with 0.2 mg/g CDs), and four CDRF groups (0.2 mg/g CDs + RF treated for 10, 20, 30, and 40 min, respectively). The packaged meatball samples were pasteurized using a RF processor (Model SO6B, 6 kW, 27 MHz, 11m, Strayfield Co. Ltd., Theale, Berkshire, UK) with the plate spacing set to 20 mm. The center temperatures of samples during the RF treatment were recorded at 5-min intervals using a fiber optic sensor. Penetration depth was 2 ± 0.2 cm. The surface temperatures of samples were monitored via the use of an infrared thermal imaging camera (Fluke Ti10 Multi-Purpose Imager, Fluke, USA) and recorded within 10 s. Microbiological analysis was used to select the most optimal CDRF heating time.

The comparison of quality variation was conducted between the control sample, CDs sample, HPS sample, and the optimal CDRF treatment sample. One part of the samples were directly used for detecting variation of color, flavor, and volatile compounds for evaluating the quality changes of freshly prepared meatballs with different treatment. The other samples were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C to evaluate the changes of POV, TBARS, TVB-N, TMA and other fishy odor components during storage, and samples were collected and measured on 0th day and 15th day.

An independent test was performed to observe the synergistic effect of the combination of CDs and RF treatment. After cooking at 100 °C for 12 min, meatballs were cooled at room temperature for 40 min. The samples were transferred to bags contained CDs dispersed water (4 mL, 2 mg/mL) and packaged. Subsequently, the meatballs with and without CDs were treated by the RF for 30 min. The samples were named RF + CDs and RF, respectively. The changes of POV, TBARS, TVB-N, TMA and viable bacterial count were evaluated.

The total number of bacteria in boiled meatball samples with different treatment was determined following Meng, Zhang, and Adhikari (2012). Ten gram of meatball sample was diluted with 90 mL of physiological saline and homogenized. The homogenate was continuously diluted with saline water in ten-fold dilutions. One milliliter of diluent with different dilution ratios were added to Petri dish and mixed with plate count agar (46 ± 1 °C). After 48 h of cultivation at 37 °C, the typical colonies were recorded.

The L* (brightness), b* (yellowness/blueness) and a* (redness/greenness) values of the crab meatballs were acquired using a portable chroma meter (Konica Minolta, CR-400, Tokyo, Japan). The sample was homogenized in advance and placed in plastic petri dishes. After calibrated by a standard white board, the chroma meter test lens was vertically placed on the surface of the homogenate to be tested, and closely attached to the homogenate surface. Six duplication of the color values was averaged.

The flavor of crab meatball samples were determined using an electronic nose (Ruifen Trading Co., China). The measurement parameters were set as follows: airflow rate was 1 L/min, cleaning time and testing time were 120 s and 100 s, respectively. The sensors and sensing types of the electronic nose are given in Table 1 .

The volatile components variation of the crab meatball samples was carried out using a Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS, TSQ Quantum XLS; Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd, Massachusetts, USA). Head-space SPME was applied to extract the volatile components of the crab meatballs using a CAR/PDMS fiber with 75 μm film thickness. Sample was minced and 3.5 g of minced sample was put into a 15-mL vial. After adding 4 μL of the internal standard (1, 2-dichlorobenzene, 0.225 mg/ml), the vial was sealed and equilibrated for 30 min at 60 °C. Chromatographic condition: The capillary column was DB-WAX (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm film thicknesses); The initial oven temperature was 40 °C, held 1min, increased to 120 °C with a rate of 8 °C/min, then increased to 230 °C with a rate of 10 °C/min, held 8 min; carrier gas was high purity helium and the flow rate was 1 mL/min. Mass spectrometry conditions: ion source temperature was 250 °C; ionization mode was EI; electron energy was 70eV; mass scan range was 33⁓500 m/z.

The compounds were identified by mass spectrometry according to the retrieval database (NIST 11 Library and Wiley 07 Library), and the compounds with matching and purity greater than 800 were screened out from the detected volatiles. By comparing the peak area with the internal standard 1, 2-dichlorobenzene, the relative amount of volatiles was determined using a response factor of 1.

POV of the crab meatball sample was measured as per the National Standards of PR China GB 5009.227–2016. Briefly, 2 g (accurate to 0.001 g) of lipid sample was mixed with the 3:2 acetic acid: chloroform solution (30 mL). Then, 1 mL of saturated KI solution was added to the mixture and shaken for 0.5 min. After the addition of 100 mL distilled water, the mixture was titrated with sodium thiosulfate solution (0.01 mol/L). The results are expressed in terms of the amount of peroxide equivalent to iodine in g/100 g.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS)
ce:section.ce:para: TBARS value of each crab meatball sample was recorded as per the National Standards of the P.R. China GB 5009.181–2016. Accurately, 50 mL of trichloroacetic acid (7.5%) was mixed with 5 g (accurate to 0.001 g) of the sample and then homogenized. The homogenate was centrifuged (3, 500×g, 20 min) and filtered. The mixture of 5 mL of thiobarbituric acid (0.02 mol/L) and 5 mL of the filtrate was reacted in a 90 °C water bath for 30 min. After cooling, the absorbance was recorded at 532 nm. A standard curve was prepared using 1,1,3,3-tetramethoxypropane. The results are expressed as mg malondialdehyde (MDA) per kg of sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N)
ce:section.ce:para:
TVB-N value was obtained based on the semi-trace determination of nitrogen as outlined in the National Standards of the P.R. China GB/T 5009.44–2003. Briefly, 20 g (accurate to 0.001 g) of the minced sample was mixed with 100 mL of deionized water. The mixture was soaked for 30 min, then filtered. Ten mL of the filtrate and 5 mL of magnesium oxide suspension (10 g/L) were distilled in the reaction chamber for 6 min. The distilled liquid was adsorbed using boric acid (20 g/L), then titrated with hydrochloric acid (HCl, 0.01 mol/L) standard solution. TVB-N value was estimated using formula (2):
(2)
X =
(
V 1
−
V 2
)
× 0.01 × 14
M ×
5 / 100
× 100

X: TVB-N value, mg/100 g; M: Sample mass, g; V
1 : The volume of the HCl solution used to titrate the sample, mL; V
2 : The volume of the blank control, mL.

TMA value of each crab meatball sample was determined by the second method (Headspace gas chromatography method) of National Standards of the P.R. China GB5009.179-2016. A gas chromatograph (Shimadzu, GC-2010 PLUS, Kyoto, Japan) fitted with DB-WAX capillary column (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm) and flame ionization detector (FID) was used to measure. Accurately, 10 g (accurate to 0.001 g) of the sample was mixed with 20 mL of trichloroacetic acid (5%) and then homogenized for 1 min. The homogenate was centrifuged (3, 500×g, 5 min) and filtered. The filtrate was diluted to 50 mL with 5% trichloroacetic acid solution. Two mL of diluent was transferred into 20 ml headspace bottle. After sealing, 5 mL of 50% sodium hydroxide solution was injected. The measurements were then carried out by gas chromatograph. A standard curve was prepared using trimethylamine standard solution.

Experiments were completely randomized in design and performed in triplicate. The experimental data were analyzed by SPSS 26.0 software. Data were subject to one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Smart Nose software was used for principal component analysis. Statistical differences among the mean values were identified using Tukey's test, p < 0.05 indicated significant difference.

It can be seen from Fig. 1 a, the colony forming units (CFUs) in samples of the CDRF20, CDRF30, and CDRF40 groups were significantly lower (p < 0.05) than those of CDs and CDRF10 groups. Compared with the control group, CDRF treated for 30min reduced the CFUs by 2.25 logarithm. The amount of CFUs retained after RF treated for 30min met requirements set forth in the National Standards of the P.R. China GB29921-2013. According to GB29921-2013, the CFUs in cooked meat products should be less than 1000 per gram, i.e., the logarithm value should be less than 3 logarithm (Xu, Zhu, Zhang, Cao, & Adhikari, 2021). While, there was no significant difference between CDRF30 group and CDRF40 group in bactericidal effect (p > 0.05). The results were similar with those obtained by Xu, Zhang, Bhandari, and Cao (2018), who reported that there was no significant different in the germicidal efficacy between ZNCRF (combination of RF and ZnO nanoparticles) pasteurization 20min and 30min for Twice-cooked pork dish samples. The CFUs in WB group was significantly lower (p < 0.05) than that of CDRF20, CDRF30, CDRF40 and HPS groups. The bactericidal effect of HPS treatment was the best as no CFUs was detected in samples.

The bactericidal effect of CDRF did not continue to increase after heating for 30min, which may be related to the temperature change of the samples. As shown in Fig. 1b, with the increase of RF heating time, the center temperature of the sample gradually rises. After heating for 30min, the central temperature of the sample rises from the beginning 27.08 °C–67.49 °C. Then, the temperature changes become stable and the temperature no longer significantly increases. Therefore, under the conditions of this study, the optimal RF heating time was 30 min. In subsequent studies, only CDRF30 treatment was tested and compared with other treatments.

Changes in the color of crab meatballs with different treatment are exhibited in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2a, in the case of a* value and b* value for homogenized samples, there was no significance between control group, CDs group and CDRF30 group. The a* and b* values in HPS treatment was significantly higher than those of the other treatments. The L* value in CDRF30 sample was significantly lower (p < 0.05) than that of control sample and CDs sample, and significantly higher (p < 0.05) than that of HPS sample. L* represents brightness of samples. The result showed that the brightness of meatballs was decreased by heating treatment, especially by HPS treatment at high temperature. Correspondingly, it can be observed from Fig. 2b that, the color of HPS sample getting darkened. Only HPS treatment gave a visible difference in color compared with control sample. In this study, it was also found that HPS treatment darkened the color of crab roe (Fig. 2c), which may be the main reason for the color variation of crab meatball samples.

Fig. 3 a shows the flavor response of the four groups. Except for the slight differences between the S5, S7, and S9 sensors, the signal values of the other sensors in CDs and CDRF30 groups have no significant changes compared with the control sample. While the signal values of S5, S6, S7, S9, and S10 of HPS sample were obviously higher than those of the control sample. It should be noted that the S5 sensor can detect ketones, aldehydes and alcohols, which are small molecules produced after lipid oxidation and one of the reasons for the fishy odor produced in aquatic products such as crab meat (N. Wu, Gu, Tao, Wang, & Ji, 2014). The signal value of the S5 sensor in control group was higher than that of CDs group and CDRF30 groups, while lower than that of HPS group. It can be deduced that during the HPS treatment, the degree of lipid oxidation increased, which promoted the production of small molecular substances such as ketones, aldehydes, and alcohols. However, in the CDs group and CDRF30 group, the lipid oxidation was inhibited due to the excellent antioxidant properties of CDs (L. Zhao et al., 2021). In order to study the effect of different treatments on the flavor characteristics of meatballs, principal component analysis was used to interpret the e-nose flavor profile data of the samples. As shown in Fig. 3b, the cumulative contribution of principal component 1 and principal component 2 is 96.7%, which indicated that the overall information of the sample can be displayed. The discrimination index (DI) value is positive, indicating that the differences in the 4 groups of crab meatball samples can be completely distinguished. In the Figure, graphs of control group, CDs group, and CDRF30 group are not far apart, indicating that although the flavors of the meatballs treated by CDs and CDRF30 have changed, the changes are not obvious. However, the graphs of the HPS sample was far apart from the other three groups, indicating that the HPS group had different flavors from the other three groups.

Changes in volatile compounds of crab meatballs with different treatment are shown in Table 2 . According to the table, 54, 50, 53 and 66 volatile compounds were identified in the control group, CDs group, CDRF30 group and HPS group, and the corresponding total concentration were 6877.84 ± 576.71, 6368.87 ± 562.79, 6415.53 ± 532.55, and 8227.43 ± 642.84 μg/kg, respectively. By comparing these four samples, it was found that the HPS sample contained some specific substances which is not contained in the other groups. The total concentration in HPS group was the largest. There was no significant difference among the control group, CDs group and CDRF30 group. The results were corresponded to the principal component analysis result of flavor analysis. Compared with control sample, although the flavors of the CDs and CDRF30 samples have been changed, the changes were not obvious. During cooking, a series of complex heat-induced reactions occur in fat tissues of pork and crab meat, accompanied produced by volatile compounds (Mottram, 1998).

A total of 7 aldehydes, 15 alcohols, and 10 ketones were isolated and identified in the crab meatball samples. Aldehydes are mainly derived from the oxidative decomposition of fat tissue, have high volatility, and are one of the main flavor substances that constitute heated meat products. Among them, pentanal, hexanal, heptanal and decanal were considered to contribute to the fishy odor in aquatic products (Guo et al., 2019), which had been detected in meat of Chinese mitten crab (Chen & Jiang, 2009). Alcohols are generally generated by the oxidative decomposition of fat or the reduction of carbonyl compounds, and because of their high threshold value, they does not contribute much to food flavor (Du et al., 2021). Ketones are generally produced through the oxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids, various Maillard, and the degradation of amino acids (Selli, Prost, & Serot, 2009). In boiled crab meatballs, 2-Nonanone and 2-heptanone were detected. These two substances were odor-active ketones, have also been detected in steamed Chinese mitten crab (N. Wu et al., 2014). The highest content of ketone in the meatball sample was 6-Methyl-5-hepten-2-one (a kind of ketene), which was 979.81 ± 48.2 μg/kg in the HPS group, remarkably higher than those in other three groups. Ketenes are produced during heating as a product of lipid oxidation (Karahadian & Lindsay, 1989).

In the control group, CDs group, CDRF30 group and HPS group, a total of 4, 3, 2, 6 aldehydes, 11, 8, 9, 10 alcohols, and 6, 5, 4, 8 ketones were detected, respectively; and the total contents of aldehydes, ketones and alcohols were 1451.57 ± 115.26, 1284.28 ± 108.38, 1410.81 ± 128.63 and 2215.69 ± 142.28 μg/kg, respectively. The total kinds of aldehydes, alcohols, and ketones in CDs group was obviously lower than that in control group. This result showed that, due to its excellent antioxidant performance, the addition of CDs could inhibit the lipid oxidation during cooking and reduce the generation of aldehydes, ketones and alcohols. The total content in CDRF30 group was slightly higher than that in CDs group, which may be due to the fact that RF heating increased the degree of lipid oxidation, resulting in the increase of total content of aldehydes, ketones and alcohols. The treatment temperature of HPS group (121 °C for 30 min) was much higher than that of CDRF30 group (27.08 °C ∼ 67.49 °C). The results showed that the total content of HPS group was the highest, indicating that the degree of lipid oxidation caused by this treatment was the highest.

A total of 16 alkanes and 23 alkenes were detected in the crab meatball samples. Hydrocarbons do not contribute much to the flavor because of their high threshold value, but the synergistic effect of alkanes and alkenes may contribute to the overall flavor of meat. The total concentration of alkanes in HPS group was obviously higher (p < 0.05) than that in control group, CDs group, and CDRF30 group. Esters are mainly produced by the interaction between lipid oxidation and free fatty acids in muscle tissue (W. Zhu et al., 2021). Only 5 kinds of ester substances were identified in the sample, which were few and accounted for a relatively small amount. Only two esters were detected in the CDRF30 group, and the total content was significantly lower than that in other groups. In addition, 2-Pentylfuran was isolated and identified from the crab meatball samples. It has been detected in cooked crab meat (Syhre, Scotter, & Chambers, 2008) and cooked meatballs (W. Zhu et al., 2021). Furans, with high threshold values, are mainly produced by lipid oxidation. 2-Pentylfuran was only detected in the CDRF30 and HPS groups, further indicating that the heating treatment will aggravate the oxidation degree of the sample.

The results of synergistic effect test were shown in Table 3 . Values of POV, TBARS and viable bacterial count in RF + CDs treatment were significantly lower (p < 0.05) than those in RF treatment. The results indicated that the addition of CDs could suppress the thermal quality deterioration by the RF treatment of heating and enhance the pasteurization effect of RF treatment.

Lipid oxidation could produce off-flavor in oxidized meat and meat products. By using CDs as antioxidant, oxidative stability of the meatballs could be successfully enhanced. The POV is an important parameter for judging the degree of oxidation of oil and fat samples. In the initial stage of oxidation, the higher the POV value, the higher the degree of oxidation of the sample. TBARS value of meatballs was assessed to monitor the levels of secondary oxidation products (represented by MDA). The POV and TBARS values of crab meatballs are shown in Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b. On the 0th day of storage, the variation trend of TBARS and POV values was consistent, and there was no significance between CDRF30 group and control group. The POV and TBARS values of the HPS sample was remarkably higher (p < 0.05) than that in the control group, indicating that the 30-min high temperature and high pressure treatment significantly increased the oxidation degree of the sample.

After the 15-day storage, both POV and TBARS values of four treatment groups all increased. This may be due to the high fat content in meatball samples, which is easily to be oxidized. As the degree of oxidation deepens, the secondary metabolites gradually accumulate, resulting in a continuous increase in the POV and TBARS values. The increase in the control group was obviously greater than that of the other groups.

Because of the antioxidant activities of CDs, the POV and TBARS values of CDs group were significantly lower (p < 0.05) than those of control group after stored for 15 days. According to existing studies, it can be explained that antioxidative CDs delayed the lipid oxidation rate in meatballs by quenching free radicals. The formation of free radicals, which is the first step in lipid oxidation, precedes the formation of lipid hydroperoxides (Das Purkayastha et al., 2014). By donating hydrogen atoms, antioxidants can convert free radicals into more stable non-free radical products (Eunok & Min, 2006; Tao, Zhang, & Yu, 2007; Wang, Zhang, & Mujumdar, 2012), thus interrupting the chain reaction of free radicals. Our previous studies have shown that CDs exhibited a large number of hydroxyl functional groups (hydrogen donor) on the surface, showing excellent performance in scavenging superoxide anion, hydroxyl and DPPH radicals. In the experiment for improving the stability of frying oil, it is proved that CDs has the desirable ability to inhibit the oxidative rancidity of frying oil (L. Zhao et al., 2021). Das Purkayastha et al. (2014) also demonstrated the capacity of CDs for scavenging DPPH radicals, and reported that the use of composite packaging contained CDs could be helpful to improve the oxidative shelf life of lipid foods.

After the 15-day storage, the values of POV and TBARS contained in CDRF30 meatballs were significantly lower (p < 0.05) than those in CDs samples. It might be caused by the inactivation of bacteria and enzyme causing the oxidation or the decomposition of POV precursor substance by the RF treatment. The POV and TBARS value of the CDRF30 group was significantly lower (p < 0.05) than those of control group and HPS group, which proved that the combined treatment of CDs and RF can effectively delay the lipid oxidation of crab meatball samples during refrigerated storage.

It can be seen from Fig. 5 a, after storage for 15 days at 4 °C, the TVB-N values of all crab meatball samples were increased, especially in the control group. TVB-N value indicates the activities of microorganisms and endogenous enzymes in the muscles of animal-derived foods, which decompose proteins and produce basic nitrogenous substances (Y. Zhao, Jiang, Xu, Wang, & Xu, 2017). During cooking, endogenous enzymes in crab meatballs are inactivated by high temperature. Therefore, the basic nitrogenous substances are mainly produced by residual microorganisms during refrigerated storage (Cachaldora, Garcia, Lorenzo, & Camino Garcia-Fontan, 2013).

Due to the antibacterial properties of CDs, the TVB-N values of CDs group and CDRF30 group were lower than that of control group after stored for 15 days. Among them, the TVB-N value of the CDRF30 group was much lower than that of the CDs group (p < 0.05), indicating the pasteurization effect of CDs and RF combined treatment was better than that of CDs alone. During refrigerated storage, the main spoilage bacteria in boild crab meatball samples with vacuum packaging are mainly lactic acid bacteria, facultative anaerobes, which have weak abilities to decompose proteins (Pexara, Metaxopoulos, & Drosinos, 2002). Because of the excellent sterilizing effect, the change of TVB-N value in HPS group was relatively smoothly during the 15-day storage period, there was no significance between the data of 0th day and 15th day. From the point of view of TVB-N, HPS treatment has the best microorganism safety. The effect of CDRF30 treatment is slightly lower than that of HPS treatment.

TMA, with a quite low odor threshold value (about 0.2 mg/kg), has been alleged to be responsible for the fishy odor in some foodstuff, including fish, fish soup, fish sauce, milk, vegetables, pork and crab (Jiang, Jin, Wu, & Wang, 2016). Under the action of microorganisms and enzymes, TMA can be produced by the degradation of TMAO, carnitine, and choline. (T. H. Wu & Bechtel, 2008). In addition, heating treatment and high pressure treatment could promote the degradation of TMAO to produce TMA (Erkan et al., 2011).

The result of TMA content of samples in 0th day and 15th day of storage were given in Fig. 2b. It can be seen from the data of 0th day, after treatment with HPS sterilization at 121 °C for 30 min, the levels of TMA of the crab meatball samples increased significantly (p < 0.05). More TMAO was converted to TMA during the course of HPS treatment. J. Zhu et al. (2012) reported that TMAO values in four kinds of gadoid fish and four kinds of squid were converted to TMA and dimethylamine (DMA) after samples were cooked at 100 °C for 15 min and 30 min. The results also showed that the heating process can promote the conversion of TMAO to TMA, and more TMA can be produced by heating for 30 min than by heating for 15 min. Similarly, Lin and Hurng (1985) demonstrated that the level of formation of DMA and TMA from TMAO by non-enzymatic pathways depended on the heating temperature. In our results, as the temperature of HPS treatment (121 °C) was higher than CDRF treated for 30 min, corresponding to the production of TMA in HPS group (160.73 μg/kg) was markedly (p < 0.05) higher than CDRF30 group (80.95 μg/kg). After stored for 15 days, the TMA level in control sample was remarkably higher (p < 0.05) than those of other groups, while the level in CDRF30 sample was lower. Combined with the analysis of TVB-N results, it can be proved that the combined treatment of CDs and RF effectively inhibited the increase of TMA value resulted by bacterial degradation of TMAO in crab meatballs during storage. The value in CDRF30 was not remarkably different from that of the HPS group. Due to the higher sterilization effect of HPS, there was no significant increase in TMA value during storage (p > 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation of pentanal, hexanal, heptanal, and decanal
ce:section.ce:para: As flavor substances that contribute to the formation of the fishy odor, the concentration changes of pentanal, hexanal, heptanal, and decanal during storage are shown in Table 4 . It can been seen from the data of 0th day, pentanal and decanal were not detected in the control group, CDs group, and CDRF30 group; Heptanal was not detected in the CDs group. The total concentration of these four aldehydes in HPS group (358.71 μg/kg) was obviously higher (p < 0.05) than that in CDs group (77.79 μg/kg), CDRF30 group (117.75 μg/kg) and control group (213.09 μg/kg). There was no significant difference between CDRF30 group and CDs group (p > 0.05). These results indicated that HPS treatment promoted the production of fishy odorants, while the addition of CDs antioxidant effectively inhibited the production of fishy odorants. After stored for 15 days, new flavor compounds were detected in the control group, CDs group and CDRF30 group except those detected on 0th day. Pentanal and decanal was detected in control group, decanal was detected CDRF30 group, pentanal and heptanal was detected in CDs group. Additionally, the concentrations of the four aldehydes also significantly increased (p < 0.05) compared with those on 0th day. The total concentration of four aldehydes was ranked as follows: control group > HPS group > CDs group > CDRF30 group. The total concentration of pentanal, hexanal, heptanal, and decanal in CDRF30 group was only 200.99 μg/kg, which was remarkably lower than those in other groups (p < 0.05). The above results further indicated that, compared with the control group, the combination of CDs and RF can effectively inhibit the formation of fishy odorants by reducing lipid oxidation, especially pentanal, hexanal, heptanal, and decanal, and can effectively reduce the production of fishy odor of crab meatballs during storage. In addition, although the oxidation rate of HPS group was the lowest during storage, the total concentration of the four aldehydes in HPS samples was significantly higher than those in CDs and CDRF30 samples at the end of storage (15th day).

Compared with control sample, CDRF30 treatment could effectively reduce the degree of lipid oxidation of boiled crab meatballs after 15-day storage at 4 °C as evidence by decreased POV value and TBARS value, reduce the generation of secondary oxidation product, especially pentanal, hexanal, heptanal and decanal which contribute to the fishy odor. The TVB-N value and TMA value in CDRF30 group were also lower, indicating that CDRF30 reduced the total number of microorganisms in the meatballs and then inhibit the formation of TMA value resulted by bacterial degradation of TMAO. HPS treatment gave different in color and flavor of boiled crab meatballs, promoted the TMAO converted to TMA during processing. The total values of pentanal, hexanal, heptanal and decanal in HPS group after 15-day storage were much higher than CDRF30 group. Therefore, it can be proved that the combination of RF and CDs can be a potential method for inhibiting the formation of fishy odor and improving the sensorial, physical and microbial qualities of boiled crab meatballs.

Although a large number of studies have shown that CDs has the potential to be applied in the food preservation field, the research on the toxicity, degradation and migration of CDs is still in its infancy and needs to be further studied in the future.

Linlin Zhao: Writing – original draft, Research and drafting performed by Linlin Zhao. Min Zhang: Oversight of the project and field experience were provided by Min Zhang and Haixiang Wang, Critical review of the manuscript was performed by Min Zhang and Haixiang Wang. Haixiang Wang: Oversight of the project and field experience were provided by Min Zhang and Haixiang Wang, Critical review of the manuscript was performed by Min Zhang and Haixiang Wang.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial supports from the China Key Research Program (Contract No. 2017YFD0400501), National First-Class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology (No. JUFSTR20180205), Yangzhou City Agricultural Key R&D Program (No.YZ2019034), the 111 Project (BP0719028), Research Innovation Project for Postgraduates in Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX20_1846), Jiangsu Province (China) “Collaborative Innovation Center for Food Safety and Quality Control” Industry Development Program, all of which enabled us to carry out this study.
